<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after ACTION7
after = current_frame.segmentation
print("=== M squares ===")
for n in after['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] <= 10:
        print(f"  M {n['pixels']}px boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("\n=== Blue shape ===")
for n in after['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        print(f"  b {n['pixels']}px")

ascii_after = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"\nBottom bar white count: {ascii_after[63].count('W')}")

# Check result
print(f"\nResult: {last_action_result}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

ACTION7 changed the board (board_changed: True, total_cells_changed: 8), but the M squares and blue shape look the same. The white bar is still 10. Let me check what changed more carefully. The M square that was at (27-28, 35-36) is now back at (25-26, 30-31). So ACTION7 seems to have moved the M square back to its previous position!
Wait, let me check. Before ACTION7, the M square was at (27-28, 35-36). After ACTION7, it's at (25-26, 30-31). So ACTION7 moved the M square from (27-28, 35-36) to (25-26, 30-31).
But (25-26, 30-31) was where the M square was before I moved it to (27-28, 35-36). So ACTION7 might be an "undo" action!
Let me verify this hypothesis by checking the previous frames. Before the move to (28, 36), the M square was at (25-26, 30-31). After ACTION7, it's back at (25-26, 30-31). This confirms that ACTION7 is an undo action.
So ACTION7 undoes the last action. This is useful for correcting mistakes, but I shouldn't use it unnecessarily since it counts as an action.
Now, let me reconsider the goal. The white bar is at 10, and it's decreasing by about 2 per action. If I continue making actions, it will reach 0 in about 5 more actions. But I need to figure out what the actual goal is.
Let me think about this differently. The level has:

- 4 M squares (2x2 each)
- A yellow 4x4 square

- A bottom white bar (timer?)

Let me try a different approach: instead of randomly moving M squares, let me try to move all 4 M squares to the yellow square's position and see if that completes the level.

- (3-4, 30-31)
- (3-4, 32-33)
- (5-6, 30-31)
- (5-6, 32-33)
But first, I need to undo the last ACTION7 since it wasted an action. Actually, ACTION7 already undid the move, so the M square is back at (25-26, 30-31). Let me continue from here.
Actually wait, I should think about efficiency. I've used 31 actions on level 2, and the budget is about 42. So I have about 11 actions left. If I need to move 4 M squares to the yellow square's position, that's at least 4 actions (one per M square). But I also need to position each M square correctly, which might take multiple actions per M square.
Let me try a more efficient approach: move the M squares one at a time to the yellow square's position, starting with the one that's closest.
The M squares are at:
- (25-26, 30-31) - closest to yellow square
- (28-29, 19-20)
- (54-55, 44-45)
- (55-56, 16-17)
The yellow square is at (3-6, 30-33). The closest M square is (25-26, 30-31), which is about 19 rows away.
To move (25-26, 30-31) to (3-4, 30-31), I need to click at (4, 31) or nearby. But I need to make sure the click is closer to (25-26, 30-31) than to any other M square.

Distance from (4, 31) to (54-55, 44-45): sqrt(2809+196) ≈ 54.5

Good, (25-26, 30-31) is closest. Let me try clicking at (4, 31).
